lessonTitle: From Prompt Engineering to Context Engineering
coreMechanism: capability expansion by moving from one-shot prompting to selective context loading inside a tiny memory budget
what: Shows why prompt-only execution breaks down as task scope expands.
how: A small working window forces the system to pull only the right files and tools into the bench at the right time.
why: Selective context loading creates stronger execution than a single oversized prompt.
realWorldAnchor: A repo-scale coding task fails when the model tries to answer everything from one instruction.
mentalModel: Two workbenches: one empty except for an instruction card, the other fed by a small intake gate that loads only the needed tools.
edgeCase: Dumping every file onto the bench overfills the workspace and creates clutter instead of progress.
primaryFlow: request -> small context gate -> prepared bench -> stable output
mainConstraint: Tiny context window / bounded work surface.
tradeoff: Better execution requires orchestration and selective loading overhead.
physicalAnalogy: A tiny workshop that upgrades from spoken instructions only to a bench fed by just-in-time tools and file cards.
labelFusion: Prompt, files, tools, window, and output labels live directly on the workbench objects.
compositionIntent: Left side shows fragile prompt-only work; right side shows a constrained intake gate feeding a prepared bench.
hotPath: request crate -> small window gate -> prepared bench -> stable output tray
spatialLayout: split comparison: weak left branch, strong right branch with central bottleneck
failureBranch: oversized full dump spills into clutter below the gate
evidenceArtifact: tiny window gate physically constrains what reaches the bench
forbiddenTemplate: same row of identical labeled boxes
visualSaliencePlan: See the small gate and prepared bench first, then notice the weak prompt-only branch, then the clutter spill.
hotPathEncoding: cyan fill on gate, bench, output tray; thick cyan arrows across the right branch
secondaryPathEncoding: prompt-only branch stays thin navy; clutter spill is red and downward
selectionReason: The lesson is about upgrading from instruction-only work to a prepared working surface with selective context loading, so a bench/world metaphor teaches the shift better than a pure filter or dispatch view.
silhouetteContrastRules: Instruction card must feel flat and fragile, the small window gate constrained, the prepared bench broad and usable, and the clutter spill loose and messy.

lessonTitle: Tool Calling, MCP, and RAG as Context Controls
coreMechanism: external capability routing that fetches only the relevant repo slice, platform action, or knowledge fragment
what: Shows how agents avoid stuffing all information into one prompt.
how: A dispatch hub routes requests to the archive, vendor desk, or knowledge library and returns only a filtered working set.
why: Selective external access keeps the live context small and useful.
realWorldAnchor: A coding agent needs code, provider actions, and outside docs without carrying everything at once.
mentalModel: A control room dispatches runners to three specialized stations instead of burying one desk in every document.
edgeCase: Directly dumping all sources into the operator desk creates overload.
primaryFlow: request -> dispatch hub -> chosen external station -> filtered working set
mainConstraint: Only relevant slices should return to the agent workspace.
tradeoff: More routing complexity buys cleaner context and better precision.
physicalAnalogy: A control room sending runners to an archive, a vendor counter, or a library.
labelFusion: Tool, MCP, and RAG labels are placed on the station signs rather than on detached captions.
compositionIntent: Central dispatch hub with three distinct outward channels and one filtered return tray.
hotPath: request board -> dispatch hub -> selected station -> working set tray
secondaryPaths: non-selected stations remain available but visually quieter
mustNotShow: three identical boxes in a row
spatialLayout: hub-and-spoke with a clear inbound request and outbound filtered tray
failureBranch: direct prompt dump falls into an overload bin below the hub
evidenceArtifact: only the tray on the far right is loaded, not the whole room
dominantMotion: inbound request, outward routing, then filtered return
forbiddenTemplate: simple triangle of labels with no dispatch semantics
visualSaliencePlan: See the dispatch hub first, then the filtered tray, then the three stations as capability options.
hotPathEncoding: cyan request board, cyan hub core, cyan return tray, and thicker inbound/return arrows
secondaryPathEncoding: station spokes are thinner navy; overload branch is red
selectionReason: The concept is primarily about routing a request to the right external capability and returning only the relevant slice, which is naturally a dispatch problem.
silhouetteContrastRules: Dispatch hub should feel central and operative, stations should feel distinct destinations, and the return tray should feel like a reduced, selected payload.

lessonTitle: The Summarization Trap in Long-Running Tasks
coreMechanism: lossy context compression removes unfinished details and produces false completion signals
what: Shows how long autonomous work drifts when detailed state is repeatedly compressed.
how: A full checklist is squeezed through a compression press into a tiny summary slip while dropped details fall out and a false-done stamp appears.
why: Compression without verification causes the agent to believe work was completed when hidden checks vanished.
realWorldAnchor: An agent spends hours on a feature, summarizes midway, and later assumes incomplete work was already verified.
mentalModel: A project checklist gets fed into a press until only a short note remains.
edgeCase: Each extra summarization loop increases the chance of losing acceptance criteria or test status.
primaryFlow: full checklist -> compression press -> summary slip -> false done state
tradeoff: Smaller state is cheaper to carry but less trustworthy.
physicalAnalogy: A manager shrinking a giant checklist into shorter and shorter notes until critical items disappear.
labelFusion: Checklist, summary, and false-done labels sit directly on the paper objects and stamp.
compositionIntent: Large-to-small pipeline with an obvious loss branch below the press.
spatialLayout: left-to-right shrinking flow with a downward red loss branch
exitPoint: right summary slip / done stamp zone
failureBranch: dropped checklist cards fall into a red missing-checks pile
evidenceArtifact: summary slip is visibly tiny compared with the original board
visualSaliencePlan: See the giant checklist and press first, then notice the tiny summary slip, then the red false-done consequences.
hotPathEncoding: thick cyan arrow into and out of the press with the summary slip centered on the main lane
secondaryPathEncoding: red dropped-detail branch and red false-done stamp dominate the consequence area
selectionReason: The lesson is about lossy compression and missing detail, so a press shrinking a checklist into a tiny summary visually communicates the fidelity loss better than a general state board.
silhouetteContrastRules: Checklist must feel large and dense, press must feel compressive, summary slip tiny, and false-done marker consequence-oriented.

lessonTitle: Harness Engineering as a Controlled Iteration Loop
coreMechanism: bounded task execution loop with fresh context, verification gates, and documentation before moving on
what: Shows the harness as an execution environment above raw prompt/context control.
how: A requirements binder feeds one task card at a time into a build-test-document loop, then resets the lane for the next card.
why: Short verified loops reduce drift and keep progress grounded in explicit artifacts.
realWorldAnchor: A product request is decomposed into small tasks that are implemented and verified one by one.
mentalModel: An assembly lane that pulls one card, works it, checks it, stamps it, and reloads fresh context.
edgeCase: Skipping the test gate sends broken work downstream.
primaryFlow: requirements binder -> task picker -> build bench -> test gate -> docs stamp -> done tray -> fresh-context hopper -> task picker
mainConstraint: Only one bounded task should be active per loop.
tradeoff: Loop discipline slows raw throughput but raises reliability and recoverability.
physicalAnalogy: A factory lane with a single-card pull system and a verification gate.
labelFusion: Task, test, docs, and reset labels live on stations and trays.
compositionIntent: Strong horizontal lane plus a visible top reset return and a red rework chute.
hotPath: task picker -> build bench -> test gate -> docs stamp -> done tray -> fresh-context hopper
secondaryPaths: rework chute from failed test back to build bench
spatialLayout: horizontal production lane with top reset loop and bottom rework branch
failureBranch: failed test diverts down and back to build bench
comparisonAxis: bounded iteration vs long unbounded run
evidenceArtifact: single task card visible on the lane
forbiddenTemplate: abstract circle arrows with no task stations
visualSaliencePlan: See the lane stations first, then the cyan reset loop, then the red rework chute.
hotPathEncoding: cyan fill on active lane stations and thick cyan arrows across the production path
secondaryPathEncoding: rework path is red and thinner; binder stays neutral
selectionReason: The real mechanism is bounded work passing through build, test, documentation, and reset, which is closer to a verified production lane than a generic loop icon.
silhouetteContrastRules: Task picker, build bench, test gate, docs stamp, and reset hopper should all read as different work stations rather than equal boxes.

lessonTitle: How Prompt, Context, and Harness Layers Fit Together
coreMechanism: stacked control architecture where harness orchestrates context and prompt rather than replacing them
what: Shows the relationship between prompt, context, and harness as layered control surfaces.
how: A theater stack places the harness in the director booth, context in the prop layer, and prompt in the script/actor layer.
why: The image explains that harnessing uses prompt and context instead of deprecating them.
realWorldAnchor: Teams wrongly assume harnessing makes prompting irrelevant, but the loop still depends on lower layers.
mentalModel: A stage where the director cues the scene, stagehands bring props, and the actor still follows the script.
edgeCase: Without the top booth the stage still has props and script but no reliable scene order.
primaryFlow: director booth -> prop/context layer -> actor following script -> output curtain
mainConstraint: Control works only when the layers cooperate instead of operating in isolation.
tradeoff: More layers increase orchestration clarity but require explicit separation of responsibilities.
physicalAnalogy: A three-layer theater stack with booth, prop deck, and performance stage.
labelFusion: Harness, context, and prompt labels are embedded on booth, prop deck, and script card.
compositionIntent: Vertical stack with top-down control and bottom-stage execution.
hotPath: harness booth -> context props -> script-guided actor -> output curtain
secondaryPaths: loose props spill when orchestration is absent
spatialLayout: vertical layer stack with stage output on the right
failureBranch: unmanaged props slide off the deck to the side
evidenceArtifact: script remains visible on the stage even with the harness booth above it
dominantMotion: top-down cueing and rightward scene output
forbiddenTemplate: three generic stacked boxes with definitions inside
visualSaliencePlan: See the top booth first, then the prop deck, then the actor and script on the stage.
hotPathEncoding: cyan booth, cue rail, prop deck highlights, and output lane to the curtain
secondaryPathEncoding: loose props spill is red and off-axis
selectionReason: The lesson is fundamentally about stacked responsibilities and top-down orchestration, which the theater stack explains more directly than a system hub.
silhouetteContrastRules: Booth should feel supervisory, prop deck shelf-like, script small and paper-like, actor organic, and curtain directional.

lessonTitle: From Cloud Agents to Autonomous Production Cycles
coreMechanism: background automation chains request intake, cloud execution, artifact review, notification, and scheduled re-runs into a repeatable loop
what: Shows harnessing as a production cycle instead of a one-off chat session.
how: An operations loop cycles through request queue, cloud workers, PR/notification, daily checks, and triggers the next run.
why: The loop makes autonomous maintenance and recurring execution practical.
realWorldAnchor: A website update workflow runs in the cloud, opens a PR, pings Slack, and rechecks daily for new model releases.
mentalModel: A night-shift operations room where the same loop keeps the system current without a human watching every minute.
edgeCase: Only real changes should escalate to a human handoff instead of generating noisy output every day.
primaryFlow: request queue -> cloud agent -> PR board -> daily watcher -> request queue
mainConstraint: The loop must keep running in the background and only surface meaningful updates.
tradeoff: Autonomy reduces manual checking but requires reliable trigger, review, and notification gates.
physicalAnalogy: A night operations room with a closed route between intake, workers, review board, and recurring patrol.
labelFusion: Slack, cloud, PR, and daily check labels are attached to the stations in the loop.
compositionIntent: Perimeter cycle around an ops board with a clearly weaker red branch to human escalation.
hotPath: request queue -> cloud agent -> PR board -> daily watcher -> request queue
spatialLayout: diamond-like perimeter loop around a central ops board
exitPoint: back into request queue / recurring loop
failureBranch: real-change escalation branches to a red human handoff gate
comparisonAxis: continuous production loop vs manual one-off runs
evidenceArtifact: the same request queue is reached again after the daily watcher station
visualSaliencePlan: See the closed cyan loop first, then the cloud agent at the top, then the red human handoff branch.
hotPathEncoding: cyan-filled perimeter stations with thick clockwise arrows
secondaryPathEncoding: human handoff branch is red and lower-right
selectionReason: The lesson is a repeating production loop with request intake, execution, review, and scheduled re-entry, so a cyclic relay/ops loop is the clearest teaching world.
silhouetteContrastRules: Request queue, cloud agent, PR board, watcher, and escalation handoff should each have different station identities so the loop does not collapse into a ring of same-size cards.
